metadata.source_document: EMA Guideline on good pharmacovigilance practices (GVP) – Module VI: Management and reporting of adverse reactions to medicinal products (Rev 2)
metadata.document_scope: Individual Case Safety Reports (ICSRs): collection, validation, classification, submission, and follow-up for medicinal products authorised in the EU

key_definitions.ICSR: Individual Case Safety Report – a report containing information on a suspected adverse reaction to a medicinal product in a single patient at a specific point in time
key_definitions.valid_ICSR: An ICSR that meets all four minimum criteria: identifiable reporter, identifiable patient, suspected medicinal product, suspected adverse reaction
key_definitions.day_zero: The date on which the minimum criteria for a valid ICSR are first brought to the attention of the MAH or NCA; this is considered day 0 and reporting timelines are calculated from this date
key_definitions.serious_adverse_reaction: A reaction that results in death, is life-threatening, requires inpatient hospitalisation or prolongation of existing hospitalisation, results in persistent or significant disability/incapacity, or is a congenital anomaly/birth defect
key_definitions.expedited_report: A valid ICSR for a serious adverse reaction that must be submitted within 15 calendar days
key_definitions.non_expedited_report: A valid ICSR for a non-serious adverse reaction that must be submitted within 90 calendar days
key_definitions.EudraVigilance: The European database for suspected adverse reactions to medicinal products authorised in the EU
key_definitions.EVPM: EudraVigilance Post-authorisation Module – for post-marketing ICSRs
key_definitions.EVCTM: EudraVigilance Clinical Trials Module – for ICSRs from interventional clinical trials (SUSARs)
key_definitions.SUSAR: Suspected Unexpected Serious Adverse Reaction in a clinical trial
key_definitions.spontaneous_report: An unsolicited communication by a healthcare professional or consumer reporting a suspected adverse reaction; does not imply a confirmed causal relationship
key_definitions.solicited_report: A report of a suspected adverse reaction obtained in an organised data collection system (e.g., clinical trials, patient support programmes, market research)
key_definitions.follow_up: Additional information received after the initial ICSR that modifies or supplements the case
key_definitions.nullification: The act of voiding an ICSR from the EudraVigilance database; can only be performed by the original sending organisation
key_definitions.amendment: A correction or update to a previously submitted ICSR that does not constitute a follow-up with new clinical information
key_definitions.MedDRA_LLT: MedDRA Lowest Level Term – the required level of coding granularity for adverse reactions in ICSRs
key_definitions.MLM: Medical Literature Monitoring – conducted by EMA under Art 27 Reg 726/2004 for centrally authorised products
key_definitions.biological_product: A medicinal product derived from biological sources requiring batch number, brand name, and active substance identification for traceability

rules.rule_text: A valid ICSR must contain all four minimum criteria simultaneously: (1) an identifiable reporter, (2) an identifiable patient, (3) a suspected medicinal product, and (4) a suspected adverse reaction. An ICSR missing any one of these four elements is not valid and cannot be submitted.
rules.source_quote: The minimum criteria for a valid ICSR are: an identifiable reporter; an identifiable patient; a suspect medicinal product; a suspected adverse reaction.
rules.exceptions: If minimum criteria are not met, the organisation should attempt to obtain the missing information. The incomplete report must still be documented in the pharmacovigilance system.

rules.rule_text: A reporter is identifiable if a first name, last name, initials, or address are available, or if the reporter's professional qualification is recorded. Anonymous reporters do not satisfy the identifiable reporter criterion.
rules.source_quote: An identifiable reporter means that the reporter can be identified by a first name, last name, initials or address or by his/her professional qualification.

rules.rule_text: A patient is identifiable if at least one of the following is available: age, age group, sex, initials, date of birth, name, or reference number. The patient does not need to be named.
rules.source_quote: An identifiable patient means that there is at least one of the following: age, age group (e.g. 'adolescent', 'adult', 'elderly'), sex, initials, date of birth, name, or reference number.

rules.rule_text: All valid ICSRs for serious suspected adverse reactions must be submitted as soon as possible and in no case later than 15 calendar days after initial receipt (day zero). This applies to both MAHs and NCAs reporting to EudraVigilance.
rules.source_quote: The submission of serious valid ICSRs is required as soon as possible, but in no case later than 15 calendar days after initial receipt.
rules.timeline: 15 calendar days from day zero
rules.exceptions: Class action lawsuit exemption may allow serious cases within 30 days upon MAH request (see GVP-VI-047). Certain fatal/life-threatening SUSARs from clinical trials have 7-day reporting to authority.

rules.rule_text: Valid ICSRs for non-serious suspected adverse reactions shall be submitted within 90 calendar days from the date of receipt (day zero). This obligation applies to EEA-sourced reports submitted to EudraVigilance.
rules.source_quote: Non-serious valid ICSRs shall be submitted... within 90 days from the date of receipt of the reports.
rules.timeline: 90 calendar days from day zero
rules.exceptions: Non-serious ICSRs from outside the EEA may be subject to different routing requirements.

rules.rule_text: The submission clock (day zero) starts on the date the minimum criteria for a valid ICSR are first brought to the attention of any personnel of the MAH or NCA, including third parties with contractual arrangements. Reporting timelines are based on calendar days. Day zero is not counted; day one is the first full calendar day after awareness.
rules.source_quote: The clock for the submission of a valid ICSR starts as soon as the information containing the minimum criteria has been brought to the attention of the marketing authorisation holder or the competent authority. This date should be considered as day zero. Reporting timelines for submission are based on calendar days.
rules.timeline: Day zero = date of first awareness of minimum criteria
rules.exceptions: For literature searches, day zero is the date the search was run if minimum criteria are present in the abstract, not the date the full article was obtained.

rules.rule_text: When significant follow-up information is received that changes the nature of the case (e.g., seriousness upgrade, new causal medicinal product), the clock for the follow-up report submission restarts from the date of receipt of that relevant follow-up information.
rules.source_quote: The clock for the submission of a follow-up report starts again from the date of receipt of the relevant follow-up information.
rules.timeline: New 15-day clock for serious, or 90-day for non-serious follow-up
rules.exceptions: Non-significant follow-up information does not restart the clock; such information may be submitted when convenient.

rules.rule_text: The submission of valid ICSRs electronically is mandatory for all competent authorities in Member States and marketing authorisation holders for all medicinal products authorised in the EU. Paper or fax submissions are not compliant.
rules.source_quote: The submission of valid ICSRs electronically, by competent authorities in Member States and marketing authorisation holders, is mandatory for all medicinal products authorised in the EU [DIR Art 107(3), Art 107a(4)].
rules.exceptions: Interim period arrangements may apply during transition to ICH-E2B(R3); consult current EudraVigilance implementation guidance.

rules.rule_text: ICSRs must be submitted in ICH-E2B(R2) or ICH-E2B(R3) electronic format as XML messages through the EudraVigilance gateway. The applicable format depends on the implementation timeline published by EMA.
rules.source_quote: ICSRs shall be submitted electronically in accordance with the international standard for electronic transmission of ICSRs as defined in the ICH-E2B guideline.
rules.exceptions: Specific interim arrangements for ICH-E2B(R3) transition apply; refer to EMA implementation guidance.

rules.rule_text: Post-authorisation ICSRs must be submitted to EudraVigilance Post-authorisation Module (EVPM) using the message receiver identifier value 'EVHUMAN'. NCAs in Member States and MAHs are the senders.
rules.source_quote: ICSRs submitted [to EVPM] with value 'EVHUMAN' in message receiver identifier.
rules.exceptions: Clinical trial SUSARs are submitted to EVCTM (EudraVigilance Clinical Trials Module), not EVPM.

rules.rule_text: All adverse reactions in ICSRs must be coded using the MedDRA terminology at the Lowest Level Term (LLT) level. The verbatim term used by the reporter must also be captured alongside the MedDRA LLT.
rules.source_quote: ICSRs must use MedDRA at the Lowest Level Term (LLT) for coding of adverse reactions.

rules.rule_text: Pharmacovigilance data and documents relating to individual authorised medicinal products must be retained by the MAH as long as the product is authorised and for at least 10 years after the marketing authorisation has ceased to exist.
rules.source_quote: Pharmacovigilance data and documents relating to individual authorised medicinal products shall be retained by the marketing authorisation holder as long as the product is authorised and for at least 10 years after the marketing authorisation has ceased to exist.
rules.timeline: Throughout authorisation life + 10 years post-expiry

rules.domain: MAH Obligation – Record All Spontaneous Reports
rules.rule_text: MAHs must record all reports of suspected adverse reactions originating from within or outside the EU, including spontaneous reports from healthcare professionals, consumers, and literature. Recording applies regardless of whether the report meets minimum validity criteria.
rules.source_quote: Marketing authorisation holders shall record all reports of suspected adverse reactions originating from within or outside the EU.

rules.rule_text: Causality assessment is not required to qualify a case as an ICSR. A spontaneous report implies at least a suspicion of a causal relationship by the reporter. MAHs must not override a reporter's causality assessment when submitting ICSRs.
rules.source_quote: A spontaneous report is an unsolicited communication by a healthcare professional or a consumer to a company, regulatory authority or other organisation... that describes one or more suspected adverse reactions in a patient who was given one or more medicinal products and that does not derive from a study or any organised data collection scheme.
rules.exceptions: For solicited reports from organised data collection (clinical trials, PSPs), causality assessment by the sponsor/MAH is required.

rules.rule_text: An adverse reaction is serious if it meets one or more of the following criteria: results in death; is life-threatening; requires inpatient hospitalisation or prolongation of existing hospitalisation; results in persistent or significant disability/incapacity; is a congenital anomaly/birth defect; is a medically important event or reaction.
rules.source_quote: An adverse reaction is serious if it results in death, is life-threatening, requires inpatient hospitalisation or prolongation of existing hospitalisation, results in persistent or significant disability or incapacity, is a congenital anomaly/birth defect, or is a medically important event or reaction.
rules.exceptions: Medically important events require clinical judgment; a predefined list of such events is published by the regulatory authorities.

rules.rule_text: Congenital anomalies, foetal death (including stillbirth), spontaneous abortion, and serious neonatal reactions are classified as serious adverse reactions and are subject to the 15-day expedited reporting timeline.
rules.source_quote: Congenital anomalies, foetal death, spontaneous abortion, and serious neonatal reactions are classified as serious adverse reactions.
rules.timeline: 15 calendar days from day zero

rules.rule_text: All reports of exposure to a medicinal product during pregnancy must be recorded and monitored for outcomes. The outcome of the pregnancy should be sought as follow-up. Even if the pregnancy outcome is uneventful, the exposure report must be recorded.
rules.source_quote: All reports of exposure to a medicinal product during pregnancy should be recorded and followed up for outcomes.
rules.exceptions: Uneventful pregnancy outcomes do not generate an ICSR for submission but must be retained in the pharmacovigilance system.

rules.rule_text: Suspected adverse reactions in breastfed infants exposed to medicinal products through breast milk must be collected and reported. Both the mother's exposure and the infant's reaction constitute the ICSR.
rules.source_quote: Suspected adverse reactions in breastfed children should be collected and reported.

rules.rule_text: ICSRs in paediatric patients (under 18 years) and elderly patients should include age, weight, and any concomitant medication where available. Paediatric ICSRs require particular attention to off-label use, dosing errors, and age-specific reactions.
rules.source_quote: ICSRs in paediatric patients and the elderly require particular attention to off-label use, dosing, and age-specific reactions.
rules.threshold: Paediatric: under 18 years; Elderly: typically 65+ years

rules.domain: Special Situations – Overdose, Abuse, Misuse, Medication Error
rules.rule_text: Reports involving overdose, abuse, misuse, or medication error associated with a medicinal product must be collected and submitted as ICSRs where an adverse reaction occurred or is suspected. Asymptomatic cases (no adverse reaction) must be documented but need not be submitted as ICSRs.
rules.source_quote: Reports of overdose, abuse, misuse and medication error should be collected and submitted as ICSRs where an adverse reaction is suspected.
rules.timeline: 15 days if serious, 90 days if non-serious
rules.exceptions: Asymptomatic overdose/misuse without adverse reaction: document but do not submit as ICSR unless required by product-specific conditions.

rules.rule_text: Suspected adverse reactions resulting from occupational exposure to a medicinal product (e.g., by healthcare workers or manufacturing staff) must be collected and reported as ICSRs where an adverse reaction is suspected.
rules.source_quote: Reports of occupational exposure should be collected and submitted as ICSRs where an adverse reaction is suspected.
rules.timeline: 15 days if serious, 90 days if non-serious

rules.rule_text: Lack of efficacy (LOE) must be reported as an ICSR when it is a suspected adverse reaction (e.g., for vaccines preventing infectious disease, contraceptives, anticoagulants where therapeutic failure causes harm). LOE must be linked to a specific medicinal product and must result in clinical harm.
rules.source_quote: Lack of efficacy should be reported as a suspected adverse reaction where it results in harm to the patient.
rules.timeline: 15 days if serious outcome; 90 days if non-serious
rules.exceptions: LOE without clinical harm does not qualify as a reportable adverse reaction unless required by product-specific obligations or risk management plan.

rules.domain: Special Situations – Quality Defect and Falsified Products
rules.rule_text: Suspected adverse reactions associated with quality defects or use of falsified medicinal products must be collected and reported as ICSRs. The quality defect or falsification must be documented in the case narrative and notified to the relevant competent authority.
rules.source_quote: Suspected adverse reactions associated with quality defects or use of falsified medicinal products should be collected and submitted as ICSRs.
rules.timeline: 15 days if serious, 90 days if non-serious

rules.rule_text: Any suspected transmission of an infectious agent via a medicinal product must be treated as a serious adverse reaction and submitted within 15 calendar days regardless of outcome severity at the time of reporting.
rules.source_quote: Any suspected transmission of an infectious agent via a medicinal product... should be submitted within 15 days.
rules.timeline: 15 calendar days (treated as serious regardless of initial clinical severity)

rules.rule_text: MAHs must conduct systematic monitoring of scientific and medical literature for ICSRs relating to their authorised medicinal products. Literature monitoring must cover all active substances (not just brand names) and use recognised databases (Medline, Embase, etc.). Monitoring must begin no later than the granting of the marketing authorisation.
rules.source_quote: Marketing authorisation holders shall monitor the scientific literature for information on suspected adverse reactions to their medicinal products.
rules.timeline: Ongoing; monitoring must start on grant of marketing authorisation
rules.exceptions: For active substances under EMA Medical Literature Monitoring (Art 27 Reg 726/2004), MAHs are exempt from submitting literature ICSRs for those substances.

rules.rule_text: MAHs must not submit literature ICSRs to EudraVigilance for active substances that are already monitored by the EMA Medical Literature Monitoring (MLM) programme. Duplicate submissions create data quality problems and are prohibited.
rules.source_quote: MAH shall only submit literature ICSRs for active substances NOT monitored by the Agency.
rules.exceptions: MAHs may still need to review EMA-monitored literature for PSUR preparation; obligation is specifically for ICSR submission to EudraVigilance.

rules.rule_text: The EMA conducts Medical Literature Monitoring (MLM) of a predefined list of active substances under centralised authorisation in accordance with Article 27 of Regulation (EC) No 726/2004. ICSRs identified through MLM are entered into EudraVigilance by EMA and transmitted to relevant MAHs.
rules.source_quote: The Agency will conduct the monitoring of the scientific and medical literature for a predefined list of active substances in accordance with Article 27 of Regulation (EC) No 726/2004.

rules.rule_text: The EMA must make all EU adverse reaction reports available to the WHO/UMC Uppsala Monitoring Centre on a weekly basis in ICH-E2B(R3) format.
rules.source_quote: The Agency makes all EU adverse reaction reports available to WHO/UMC weekly in ICH-E2B(R3) format.
rules.timeline: Weekly

rules.rule_text: An ICSR submitted to EudraVigilance can only be nullified by the original sending organisation. Once nullified, the case cannot be reactivated. Individual versions of a case cannot be nullified – nullification applies to the entire case. Nullification must be justified and documented.
rules.source_quote: Only the original sending organisation can nullify; once nullified cannot be reactivated; individual versions cannot be nullified, only entire case.
rules.exceptions: Nullification is permitted where a case is a confirmed duplicate or where the case was submitted in error (e.g., does not contain a valid adverse reaction).

rules.rule_text: A case narrative shall be provided for all serious ICSRs where possible. Non-serious cases are exempt from the case narrative requirement. The narrative should summarise the clinical course of the event, relevant medical history, and assessment.
rules.source_quote: A case narrative... shall be provided, where possible, for all cases with the exception of non-serious cases.
rules.exceptions: Non-serious ICSRs are exempt from mandatory case narrative.

rules.rule_text: ICSRs should be completed and submitted in English unless otherwise requested by the receiving competent authority. Free-text fields, including case narratives, must be in English for submissions to EudraVigilance.
rules.source_quote: The ICSR should be completed and submitted in English if not otherwise requested.
rules.exceptions: NCAs may accept national language ICSRs in their national database; submissions to EudraVigilance require English.

rules.rule_text: MAHs may request a temporary exemption from the 15-day serious ICSR reporting requirement for cases arising from class action lawsuits. If granted, such cases must be submitted within 30 calendar days. The exemption must be formally requested from and approved by the relevant NCA.
rules.source_quote: MAH may request exemption to submit serious cases [from class action lawsuits] within 30 days instead of 15.
rules.threshold: 30 days (extended from 15 days) if exemption granted
rules.timeline: 30 calendar days from day zero (with NCA-approved exemption)
rules.exceptions: Exemption must be formally requested and approved; default is 15-day rule.

rules.rule_text: For ICSRs involving biological medicinal products, the brand name, active substance name, AND batch/lot number must all be present and identifiable to enable traceability. If more than one batch number is involved, the batch coinciding with the adverse reaction goes in the main drug section; additional batch numbers go in the case narrative.
rules.source_quote: For biological medicinal products, the brand name, active substance, and batch number are all required to be present and identifiable.
rules.threshold: All three identifiers (brand name + active substance + batch number) required
rules.exceptions: If batch number is missing, MAH/NCA must attempt follow-up to obtain it from the reporter.

rules.rule_text: All ICSRs must be checked against existing records before submission to identify and prevent duplicate submissions. Where a duplicate is detected, the later submission must not be sent, or if already sent, one must be nullified. Duplicates identified from EMA's MLM activities must also be checked.
rules.source_quote: Literature cases should be checked to prevent the submission of duplicates ICSRs, and previously reported cases should be identified as such when reported.

rules.rule_text: Reports of suspected adverse reactions received through Patient Support Programmes (PSPs) are considered solicited reports and must be collected, processed, and submitted as ICSRs in the same manner as other solicited reports. The MAH is responsible for these reports.
rules.source_quote: Reports received through patient support programmes should be treated as solicited reports and submitted as ICSRs.
rules.timeline: 15 days if serious, 90 days if non-serious

rules.rule_text: Adverse reaction reports received through market research programmes sponsored or conducted by an MAH must be captured and submitted as ICSRs. The MAH is responsible regardless of whether a third party conducts the market research.
rules.source_quote: Reports received through market research programmes sponsored by a marketing authorisation holder must be captured and submitted.
rules.timeline: 15 days if serious, 90 days if non-serious

rules.rule_text: MAHs and competent authorities must perform risk-based audits of their ICSR pharmacovigilance systems at regular intervals to ensure compliance with GVP requirements. Audit findings must be documented and corrective actions implemented.
rules.source_quote: MAH and competent authorities shall perform risk-based audits at regular intervals.
rules.timeline: Regular intervals (risk-based frequency)

rules.rule_text: EudraVigilance sends an electronic Acknowledgement (ACK) message to ICSR senders within 2 business days of receipt. ACK code 01 indicates a valid accepted message; ACK code 02 indicates a non-valid case; ACK code 03 indicates an incorrectly formatted message. Senders must process ACK messages and correct rejected cases.
rules.source_quote: The acknowledgement message created is transmitted to the case sender, no later than 2 business days following receipt of the case. A valid message will have an ACK code 01. A non-valid message will have an ACK code 02 (if a case contained therein is non-valid) or 03 (if the message itself is not correctly formatted).
rules.timeline: Sender must correct and resubmit within original regulatory timelines

rules.rule_text: Follow-up information is classified as 'significant' if it changes the seriousness classification, outcome, causal medicinal product identification, or another key parameter of the case. Significant follow-up requires a new report submission within the applicable timeline. Non-significant follow-up may be included in the next regularly scheduled submission.
rules.source_quote: Follow-up information is significant if it changes the nature of the case. Significant follow-up information triggers a new submission requirement.
rules.timeline: Significant follow-up: 15 days (serious) or 90 days (non-serious) from receipt of follow-up information

rules.rule_text: When a previously submitted ICSR contains errors not related to new clinical information (e.g., data entry errors, coding corrections, administrative changes), an amendment report must be submitted. An amendment does not reset the reporting clock and is a correction to the existing case.
rules.source_quote: An amendment is a correction to a previously submitted ICSR that does not constitute new clinical information.
rules.exceptions: If the amendment involves new clinical information that changes the case, it should be submitted as a follow-up, not an amendment.

rules.rule_text: NCAs in Member States have access to all ICSRs in EudraVigilance that are relevant to products authorised in their territory. NCAs must process incoming ICSRs from EudraVigilance and enter them into their national pharmacovigilance database.
rules.source_quote: Competent authorities in Member States have access to all ICSRs in EudraVigilance relevant to products authorised in their territory.

rules.rule_text: NCAs must transmit serious ICSRs received from healthcare professionals, consumers, and MAHs to EudraVigilance. The NCA must transmit within the 15-day timeline. NCAs must not retransmit ICSRs already received from EudraVigilance to avoid duplicates.
rules.source_quote: Competent authorities shall transmit serious ICSRs to EudraVigilance within 15 calendar days.
rules.timeline: 15 calendar days for serious ICSRs
rules.exceptions: NCAs must not retransmit ICSRs already in EudraVigilance to prevent duplicate entry.

rules.rule_text: NCAs are required to transmit non-serious ICSRs from the EEA to EudraVigilance within 90 calendar days. Non-serious ICSRs from outside the EEA may be stored nationally without transmission to EudraVigilance unless required by specific arrangements.
rules.source_quote: Competent authorities shall transmit non-serious ICSRs from within the EEA to EudraVigilance within 90 days.
rules.timeline: 90 calendar days from day zero
rules.exceptions: Non-serious EEA ICSRs received from NCAs outside the Member State's territory may have different routing.

rules.rule_text: MAH pharmacovigilance obligations under GVP Module VI apply to all medicinal products authorised in the EU/EEA, regardless of the country where the adverse reaction occurred. This includes products authorised nationally and via centralised procedure.
rules.source_quote: The obligations apply to all medicinal products authorised in the EU/EEA.
rules.exceptions: Investigational medicinal products in clinical trials are subject to clinical trial legislation (SUSAR rules), not GVP Module VI post-marketing obligations.

rules.rule_text: MAHs must submit serious ICSRs from non-EEA countries (third countries) to EudraVigilance within 15 calendar days where the active substance is authorised in the EU. The country of occurrence determines the relevant NCA to notify.
rules.source_quote: Marketing authorisation holders shall report serious suspected adverse reactions occurring in third countries within 15 calendar days to EudraVigilance.
rules.timeline: 15 calendar days from day zero
rules.exceptions: Non-serious reports from non-EEA countries: MAH obligation varies; consult specific NCA requirements.

rules.rule_text: In addition to EudraVigilance submission, MAHs must directly submit ICSRs to the competent authority of the Member State on whose territory the adverse reaction occurred, within the same 15-day (serious) or 90-day (non-serious) timeframes.
rules.source_quote: Marketing authorisation holders shall submit ICSRs to the competent authority of the Member State on whose territory the adverse reaction occurred.
rules.timeline: 15 days (serious) / 90 days (non-serious)
rules.exceptions: If country of occurrence is unknown, the country of the primary source (reporter) is used as a proxy.

rules.rule_text: Reports from organised data collection systems (clinical trials, patient registries, PSPs, market research, post-authorisation safety studies) are solicited and require causality assessment by the sponsor. Spontaneous reports from unsolicited communications by healthcare professionals or consumers imply suspicion of causality without explicit assessment.
rules.source_quote: Reports from organised data collection systems are considered solicited. Spontaneous reports from unsolicited communications by healthcare professionals or consumers imply suspicion of causality.

rules.rule_text: MAHs and NCAs must ensure ICSRs are as complete as possible. Where information is missing, the sender must actively seek follow-up from the reporter. The inability to obtain follow-up information must be documented in the case.
rules.source_quote: Where information is missing, the sender must actively seek follow-up from the reporter. The inability to obtain follow-up information must be documented.

rules.rule_text: Patient and reporter personally identifiable information (PII) must be pseudonymised before submission to EudraVigilance. Names must not appear in ICSRs submitted to the regulatory system. Pseudonymisation does not mean the information cannot be re-identified by the sender for follow-up purposes.
rules.source_quote: Patient and reporter personally identifiable information must be pseudonymised before submission to EudraVigilance.
rules.exceptions: The sender retains the link between the pseudonymised data and real PII for follow-up and audit purposes.

rules.rule_text: Suspected Unexpected Serious Adverse Reactions (SUSARs) from interventional clinical trials must be submitted to the EudraVigilance Clinical Trials Module (EVCTM), not EVPM. Fatal or life-threatening SUSARs must be submitted within 7 calendar days; all other SUSARs within 15 calendar days.
rules.source_quote: SUSARs from interventional clinical trials shall be submitted to EVCTM within 7 days (fatal/life-threatening) or 15 days (all others).
rules.timeline: 7 calendar days (fatal/life-threatening SUSARs); 15 calendar days (all other SUSARs)
rules.exceptions: Post-marketing ICSRs go to EVPM; only interventional trial SUSARs go to EVCTM.

rules.domain: Reporting Scope – Authorised vs Unauthorised Use
rules.rule_text: MAHs must report suspected adverse reactions regardless of whether the product was used within or outside its authorised indication (off-label use), at the authorised dose, or by the authorised route of administration.
rules.source_quote: Suspected adverse reactions must be reported regardless of whether the product was used within or outside its authorised indication.
rules.timeline: 15 days (serious) / 90 days (non-serious)

rules.rule_text: Each ICSR must be assigned a Worldwide Unique Case Identification Number (WUCIN) by the sending organisation, which is maintained throughout all follow-up and amendment submissions. The WUCIN is structured as country code + organisation identifier + sequential case number.
rules.source_quote: Each ICSR must be assigned a Worldwide Unique Case Identification Number by the sending organisation.

rules.rule_text: Each ICSR submission (initial and all follow-ups) must carry a sequential version number. The initial report is version 1. Each subsequent submission of the same case increments the version number. Version numbers must not be reused or skipped.
rules.source_quote: Each ICSR submission must carry a sequential version number. The initial report is version 1.

rules.rule_text: The professional qualification of the primary reporter must be captured in the ICSR (e.g., physician, pharmacist, other healthcare professional, consumer/non-healthcare professional, lawyer). Reporter qualification affects how the case is weighted in signal detection.
rules.source_quote: The professional qualification of the primary reporter must be captured in the ICSR.

rules.rule_text: The outcome of the adverse reaction at the time of the report must be recorded using the standardised categories: recovered/resolved; recovering/resolving; not recovered/not resolved; recovered/resolved with sequelae; fatal; unknown.
rules.source_quote: The outcome of the adverse reaction at the time of the report must be recorded using the standardised outcome categories.

rules.rule_text: Where the patient outcome is death, the date of death and the cause of death (as MedDRA LLT) must be recorded. An autopsy report must be sought as follow-up where available. Death from the suspected adverse reaction must be clearly distinguished from death from the underlying disease.
rules.source_quote: Where the outcome is death, the date and cause of death must be recorded. An autopsy report should be sought where available.

rules.rule_text: Records of all literature searches must be maintained, including the search strategy, databases used, date the search was run, and results. Records must be retained in accordance with the 10-year data retention requirement. Demonstrating due diligence in literature search is a regulatory expectation during inspections.
rules.source_quote: Records of literature searches should be maintained in accordance with Article 12 of the Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) No 520/2012.
rules.timeline: Records retained for at least 10 years

rules.domain: Literature Monitoring – Day Zero for Literature
rules.rule_text: For literature-sourced ICSRs, day zero is the date the search was run (if minimum criteria are present in the abstract), not the date the full article was obtained or reviewed by the MAH. Where a third party runs the search, clock start is the earlier of: date third party becomes aware, or date MAH becomes aware.
rules.source_quote: Day zero is the date on which an organisation becomes aware of a publication containing the minimum information for an ICSR to qualify for submission. For weekly literature searching, day zero for the submission of an adverse reaction present in an abstract is taken to be the date on which the search was conducted.
rules.timeline: Day zero = date search was run if minimum criteria in abstract
rules.exceptions: If the abstract does not contain minimum criteria but the full article does, day zero is when the full article is obtained and reviewed.

rules.rule_text: Where an MAH contracts a third party to conduct literature searching, responsibility for ICSR submission remains with the MAH. The contractual arrangement must be clearly documented. Day zero is the earlier of the date the third party becomes aware or the date the MAH becomes aware.
rules.source_quote: The responsibility for the performance of the search and subsequent submission of ICSRs still remains [with the MAH]. The transfer of a pharmacovigilance task or function should be detailed in a contract between the organisation and the service provider.
rules.timeline: Clock starts at earliest awareness (MAH or contractor)

rules.rule_text: Restricting literature searches to English-language publications only is generally not acceptable for pharmacovigilance purposes. Search limits that restrict to English language must be justified in the context of the purpose of the search.
rules.source_quote: The use of limits that reduce the search result to only those published in the English language is generally not acceptable.
rules.exceptions: Justification required if English-only searches are applied; must demonstrate no material loss of relevant ICSRs.

rules.rule_text: Under ICH-E2B(R2), literature articles reportable to the EMA must be provided in PDF format and sent via email to EVLIT@ema.europa.eu. The file name must match exactly the World-Wide Unique Case Identification Number assigned to the individual case in the E2B(R2) ICSR format.
rules.source_quote: Literature articles reportable to the Agency should be provided in PDF format and sent via e-mail to the following e-mail address: EVLIT@ema.europa.eu.
rules.exceptions: Under ICH-E2B(R3), articles are attached directly to the ICSR as document attachments per section 3.5 of the ICH-E2B(R3) Implementation Guide.

rules.rule_text: Under ICH-E2B(R3), when a literature article is submitted as an attachment, the citation must be in Vancouver style in data element C.4.r.1 'Literature Reference(s)', including DOI where available. The electronic version of the article must be attached in data element C.4.r.2 'Included Documents'.
rules.source_quote: When a literature article is sent as an attachment, the literature citation in Vancouver style is captured in data element C.4.r.1 'Literature Reference(s)'. The Digital Object Identifier (DOI) for the article should be included where available.

rules.rule_text: When a literature article describes suspected adverse reactions in more than one patient, a separate ICSR must be created for each identifiable patient. All related ICSRs from the same article must be cross-referenced using the 'Identification number of the report which is linked to this report' data element. Only one copy of the article needs to be sent.
rules.source_quote: ICSRs should be created and submitted for each individual identifiable patient described in the literature article. All ICSRs which relate to the same literature article should be cross referenced.
rules.exceptions: Only the first case submitted from the article needs to carry cross-references to all related cases; subsequent cases only need to reference the first case.

rules.rule_text: The start date (and where available, end date) of the adverse reaction must be captured. Where an exact date is not known, at minimum the year and/or month must be recorded. Approximate dates are acceptable.
rules.source_quote: The start date and end date of the adverse reaction must be captured where available.

rules.domain: Data Elements – Drug Start and Stop Date
rules.rule_text: The start date and stop date of the suspect medicinal product must be recorded where available. The time to onset of the adverse reaction (calculated from drug start to reaction start) is a key causality assessment element and must be derivable from the data.
rules.source_quote: The start date and stop date of the suspect medicinal product must be recorded where available.

rules.rule_text: The dose, dose unit, and route of administration of the suspect medicinal product must be captured in the ICSR. Where the dose is unknown, this must be explicitly noted. Dose information is critical for dose-response analysis.
rules.source_quote: The dose, dose unit and route of administration of the suspect medicinal product must be captured.

rules.rule_text: All concomitant medications taken by the patient at the time of the adverse reaction must be listed in the ICSR. Drug-drug interactions require complete concomitant medication listings. Where the medication list is incomplete, follow-up should be sought.
rules.source_quote: All concomitant medications taken at the time of the adverse reaction must be listed.

rules.rule_text: Relevant medical history and concurrent conditions must be captured in the ICSR. At minimum, any conditions that may be relevant to the adverse reaction or that represent potential confounders must be recorded.
rules.source_quote: Relevant medical history and concurrent conditions must be captured.

rules.domain: Expedited Reporting – Both Serious and Unexpected vs Expected
rules.rule_text: The 15-day expedited reporting obligation applies to all serious adverse reactions regardless of whether they are listed in the Summary of Product Characteristics (SmPC) – i.e., both expected (labelled) and unexpected (unlabelled) serious reactions require expedited reporting post-authorisation.
rules.source_quote: The submission of serious valid ICSRs is required as soon as possible, but in no case later than 15 calendar days. This applies irrespective of whether the reaction is listed in the SmPC.
rules.timeline: 15 calendar days
rules.exceptions: In pre-authorisation/clinical trial context, only unexpected serious reactions (SUSARs) require expedited reporting. Post-authorisation: all serious reactions.

rules.rule_text: When an MAH uses a contract research organisation (CRO), co-marketing partner, or other third party that may receive adverse reaction reports on the MAH's behalf, the MAH must ensure that contractual agreements clearly specify pharmacovigilance responsibilities and reporting procedures. Day zero is triggered at the earlier of the third party's or MAH's awareness.
rules.source_quote: Marketing authorisation holders shall ensure that contractual agreements clearly specify pharmacovigilance responsibilities and reporting procedures where third parties may receive adverse reaction reports.
rules.timeline: Day zero at earliest awareness (MAH or contractor)

rules.rule_text: Reports of suspected adverse reactions from consumers (non-healthcare professionals) are valid and must be accepted by MAHs and NCAs. Consumer reports count as identifiable reporters. MAHs may not refuse or discount consumer reports solely on the basis that the reporter is not a healthcare professional.
rules.source_quote: Reports of suspected adverse reactions from consumers are valid and must be accepted.

rules.rule_text: Medically important events are those that may not result in death, hospitalisation, or disability immediately but that jeopardise the patient or require intervention to prevent one of the other serious outcomes. Such events are serious per regulatory definition and subject to 15-day expedited reporting.
rules.source_quote: Medically important events are those that may not result in death or hospitalisation immediately but may jeopardise the patient and may require intervention to prevent serious outcome.
rules.timeline: 15 calendar days
rules.exceptions: Examples include drug abuse, drug dependence, drug misuse, intentional overdose without harm (if significant clinical concern).

rules.rule_text: All ICSR messages received by EudraVigilance undergo automated technical validation against EudraVigilance Business Rules. Messages failing validation receive a negative ACK (code 02 or 03). The sender is responsible for correcting and resubmitting failed messages within the original regulatory timelines.
rules.source_quote: Every message that is received in EV is validated against the EudraVigilance Business Rules and an Acknowledgement message (ACK) is created specifying whether or not the message and the case(s) therein are valid.
rules.timeline: Correction must be within original 15-day or 90-day timeline

rules.rule_text: Suspected drug-drug interaction cases that result in an adverse reaction must be reported as ICSRs. All suspect drugs in the interaction should be listed as suspect medicinal products in the ICSR, not as concomitant medications.
rules.source_quote: In drug interaction cases, all suspect drugs should be listed as suspect medicinal products.
rules.timeline: 15 days (if serious) / 90 days (if non-serious)

rules.rule_text: The indication (reason for use) of the suspect medicinal product must be captured in the ICSR using MedDRA coding. Off-label indications must be recorded as reported; the MAH must not change the indication to an authorised one if the reporter specified off-label use.
rules.source_quote: The indication for use of the suspect medicinal product must be captured using MedDRA coding.

rules.rule_text: The source type of the ICSR (spontaneous, literature, study, regulatory authority) must be accurately classified and recorded. Incorrect source type classification can affect signal detection weighting and reporting obligations.
rules.source_quote: The source type of the ICSR must be accurately classified and recorded.

rules.rule_text: The country of the primary source (reporter) must be captured in the ICSR. This is used to determine the relevant NCA and to route the case appropriately. Where the country of occurrence differs from the country of the primary source, both must be recorded.
rules.source_quote: The country of the primary source must be captured. Where the country of occurrence differs, both must be recorded.

rules.rule_text: NCAs must monitor the scientific and medical literature for ICSRs relevant to medicinal products authorised in their Member State. NCAs are responsible for entering literature-identified ICSRs into EudraVigilance where the MAH has not done so and the active substance is not under EMA MLM.
rules.source_quote: Competent authorities must monitor the scientific and medical literature for ICSRs relevant to medicinal products authorised in their Member State.
rules.exceptions: For active substances under EMA MLM programme, NCAs may rely on EMA submissions.

rules.rule_text: ICSRs stored in EudraVigilance undergo duplicate detection and MedDRA recoding as part of data quality processes before becoming available for signal detection and pharmacovigilance analyses. Senders must use current MedDRA versions.
rules.source_quote: Following duplicate detection and recoding, cases will be available for signal detection and data quality analyses.

rules.rule_text: If a non-serious ICSR is upgraded to serious based on follow-up information, the follow-up report must be submitted within 15 calendar days from the date of receipt of the information that changed the seriousness classification. The 90-day timeline no longer applies.
rules.source_quote: If follow-up information upgrades an ICSR from non-serious to serious, the follow-up must be submitted within 15 calendar days from the date of receipt of the upgrading information.
rules.timeline: 15 calendar days from receipt of seriousness upgrade information

rules.rule_text: For ICSRs where the case is incomplete or outcome unknown, the MAH must actively seek additional information from the reporter. At least one follow-up attempt must be documented. The inability to obtain information must be noted in the case.
rules.source_quote: For incomplete cases or where outcome is unknown, the MAH must actively seek additional information. At least one follow-up attempt must be documented.
rules.exceptions: Where reporter explicitly refuses further contact, this refusal must be documented.

rules.rule_text: MAHs must have procedures in place to receive and process ICSRs from non-EEA affiliates, licensees, and co-marketing partners. Contractual arrangements must include pharmacovigilance data sharing agreements. All partners must be informed of their obligation to report.
rules.source_quote: MAHs must have procedures to receive and process ICSRs from non-EEA affiliates and partners.

rules.rule_text: All pharmacovigilance processes for ICSR collection, processing, quality management, and submission must be described in the Pharmacovigilance System Master File (PSMF). The PSMF must be maintained current and available for inspection.
rules.source_quote: All pharmacovigilance processes for ICSR collection, processing, and submission must be described in the PSMF.

rules.domain: Report Type – Initial vs Follow-Up vs Amendment
rules.rule_text: Each ICSR must be correctly classified as: initial report (first report of the case), follow-up report (new significant clinical information for an existing case), or amendment (administrative correction to an existing report). The report type determines how the system processes and merges the case.
rules.source_quote: Each ICSR must be correctly classified as initial report, follow-up report, or amendment.

rules.domain: EMA – EudraVigilance Access for Healthcare Professionals
rules.rule_text: EMA makes anonymised ICSR data from EudraVigilance publicly accessible to healthcare professionals and the public through the EudraVigilance public website. The format and scope of publicly accessible data must be maintained in accordance with the EMA access policy.
rules.source_quote: EMA makes anonymised ICSR data publicly accessible to healthcare professionals and the public through the EudraVigilance website.

rules.rule_text: The person reviewing literature search results for pharmacovigilance purposes must be trained to identify relevant ICSRs. Quality control checks must be performed on a sample of literature reviews to verify the reviewer is correctly identifying relevant articles.
rules.source_quote: It is recommended that quality control checks are performed on a sample of literature reviews / selection of articles to check the primary reviewer is identifying the relevant articles.
rules.threshold: QC on a sample of literature reviews

rules.domain: Literature Monitoring – No Brand Name Only Restriction
rules.rule_text: Literature searches must be conducted using active substance names (all variants and spellings) as the primary search term, not brand names only. Searches restricted to brand names will miss ICSRs reported under generic names and are not compliant.
rules.source_quote: Searches should be performed to find records for active substances and not for brand names only.

rules.domain: Literature Monitoring – Special Report Types in Searches
rules.rule_text: Literature search strategies must not routinely exclude records for asymptomatic overdose, medication error, misuse, abuse, occupational exposure, or uneventful pregnancy. Omitting these terms is a common deficiency found during CA inspections.
rules.source_quote: Deficiencies found frequently during Competent Authority inspections include: the omission of terms to include special types of reports which needs to be addressed... Reports of asymptomatic overdose, medication error, misuse, abuse, occupational exposure; Reports of uneventful pregnancy.

rules.domain: Reporting – No Refusal of Valid Reports
rules.rule_text: MAHs must not refuse to accept or record a suspected adverse reaction report on the grounds that the report is incomplete, the reporter is unknown, or the causal relationship is uncertain. All reports meeting minimum criteria must be processed.
rules.source_quote: MAHs must not refuse to accept or record a suspected adverse reaction report on the grounds that the report is incomplete or that the causal relationship is uncertain.

rules.rule_text: All reports of suspected adverse reactions received by any employee of the MAH through any channel (including sales representatives, medical affairs, regulatory affairs, call centres) must be processed through the pharmacovigilance system. Company employees are considered part of the MAH's awareness.
rules.source_quote: All reports received by any employee of the MAH through any channel must be processed through the pharmacovigilance system.
rules.timeline: Day zero triggered at employee awareness

rules.rule_text: MAHs must monitor company-sponsored websites, social media, and digital platforms for reports of suspected adverse reactions. Reports identified through these channels must be processed as ICSRs where minimum criteria are met. Unsolicited reports on third-party websites are generally not reportable unless the MAH is made aware.
rules.source_quote: MAHs must monitor company-sponsored websites and digital platforms for reports of suspected adverse reactions.
rules.timeline: Day zero = date of awareness from digital channel
rules.exceptions: Third-party websites not associated with or sponsored by the MAH: not a mandatory monitoring obligation, but MAH must process if drawn to their attention.

rules.rule_text: When an NCA receives a serious ICSR, it must forward the ICSR to EudraVigilance and to other relevant NCAs as required. NCAs must not withhold or delay forwarding of valid serious ICSRs within the 15-day timeline.
rules.source_quote: Competent authorities must forward serious ICSRs to EudraVigilance within the 15-day timeline.
rules.timeline: 15 calendar days

rules.rule_text: MAHs and NCAs must have documented Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) covering all aspects of ICSR management including receipt, triage, data entry, medical assessment, submission, follow-up, quality control, and archiving. SOPs must be regularly reviewed and updated.
rules.source_quote: MAHs and NCAs must have documented SOPs covering all aspects of ICSR management.
rules.timeline: SOPs must be reviewed at regular intervals

rules.rule_text: Upon receipt of a potential ICSR, an initial triage must be performed to determine: (1) whether the report meets minimum ICSR validity criteria; (2) seriousness classification; and (3) applicable reporting timeline. Triage must be completed promptly to ensure timelines are met.
rules.source_quote: Upon receipt of a potential ICSR, an initial triage must be performed to determine validity, seriousness, and applicable timeline.
rules.timeline: Triage must be completed promptly after receipt

rules.rule_text: The QPPV (Qualified Person Responsible for Pharmacovigilance) is responsible for the establishment, maintenance, and oversight of the pharmacovigilance system, including ICSR collection, processing, and submission. The QPPV must be informed of systemic pharmacovigilance failures (e.g., persistent ACK errors).
rules.source_quote: If a sender fails to correct cases, then this information should be incorporated into data quality assessments and the appropriate committees should be informed.

rules.domain: Reporting – Named Patient and Compassionate Use
rules.rule_text: Suspected adverse reactions occurring in patients receiving medicinal products under named patient, compassionate use, or early access schemes must be collected and submitted as ICSRs following the same rules as post-authorisation spontaneous reports.
rules.source_quote: Suspected adverse reactions in named patient, compassionate use, or early access schemes must be submitted following the same rules as post-authorisation spontaneous reports.
rules.timeline: 15 days (serious) / 90 days (non-serious)

rules.rule_text: Serious adverse reactions identified from non-interventional post-authorisation safety studies (PASS) must be collected and submitted as ICSRs. The study design (non-interventional) does not exempt from ICSR reporting obligations.
rules.source_quote: Serious adverse reactions from non-interventional post-authorisation safety studies must be submitted as ICSRs.
rules.timeline: 15 calendar days for serious
rules.exceptions: Non-serious ICSRs from PASS: may be collected for aggregate reporting in study report rather than individual ICSR submission, depending on study protocol.

rules.rule_text: Patient age or age group at time of adverse reaction onset must be captured in the ICSR. If exact age is unknown, age group classification (neonate, infant, child, adolescent, adult, elderly) must be provided where determinable. Age information is mandatory for paediatric case analysis.
rules.source_quote: Patient age or age group at time of adverse reaction onset must be captured.

rules.rule_text: Patient sex (male/female) must be recorded in the ICSR where known. For intersex or gender-fluid patients, the biological sex relevant to the adverse reaction should be captured. Unknown sex must be explicitly coded as 'Unknown', not left blank.
rules.source_quote: Patient sex must be recorded where known. Unknown sex must be explicitly coded as 'Unknown'.

rules.rule_text: Fatal adverse reactions must be submitted as expedited 15-day serious reports. Where the cause of death is the suspected adverse reaction, the case must clearly indicate 'fatal' as the outcome and include available cause-of-death information.
rules.source_quote: Fatal adverse reactions must be submitted as expedited 15-day serious reports.
rules.timeline: 15 calendar days

rules.domain: Reporting – Aggregate Signal to Individual Cases
rules.rule_text: Where an MAH identifies a new safety signal from aggregate data analysis that involves individual case reports not yet submitted to EudraVigilance, those underlying ICSRs must be submitted immediately to the regulatory system, not held pending further analysis.
rules.source_quote: Where a new safety signal is identified from aggregate analysis involving unsupported individual cases, those cases must be submitted immediately.
rules.timeline: Immediate submission; 15-day if serious

rules.rule_text: ICSRs for vaccines must follow the same minimum validity criteria and reporting timelines as other medicinal products. Vaccine-specific data elements (vaccine brand, batch/lot number, site of injection, schedule adherence) should be captured to enable pharmacovigilance analysis.
rules.source_quote: ICSRs for vaccines must follow the same minimum validity criteria and timelines. Vaccine-specific data elements including batch number must be captured.
rules.timeline: 15 days (serious) / 90 days (non-serious)

rules.rule_text: Where a foetus is exposed to a medicinal product (through maternal use), the foetus/neonate is considered the patient for the purpose of reporting any adverse reaction. The mother's report is the source. Both mother and foetus/neonate data elements should be captured.
rules.source_quote: Where a foetus is exposed through maternal use, the foetus/neonate is considered the patient for reporting adverse reactions.

rules.rule_text: Literature that is produced or sponsored by an MAH must be reviewed for ICSRs and submitted as required, potentially in advance of formal publication. If ICSRs from such literature are brought to the MAH's attention, they must be processed in the same way as database search ICSRs.
rules.source_quote: Literature that is produced or sponsored by a marketing authorisation holder should be reviewed, so that any valid reportable ICSRs can be submitted as required in advance of publication.
rules.timeline: 15 days (serious) / 90 days (non-serious) from awareness

rules.rule_text: Literature monitoring and ICSR submission obligations apply to all products with a marketing authorisation, irrespective of whether the product is actively being marketed or commercially available. Authorisation holders of dormant or withdrawn products remain obligated until the authorisation ceases.
rules.source_quote: Literature searches should be conducted for all products with a marketing authorisation, irrespective of commercial status.
rules.exceptions: Post-marketing authorisation expiry: 10-year data retention but no ongoing monitoring obligation.

icsr_submission_timeline.description: Decision tree for determining applicable ICSR submission timeline

steps.question: Does the report meet all 4 minimum validity criteria (identifiable reporter + patient + suspected product + suspected reaction)?
steps.if_no: Document in pharmacovigilance system; seek follow-up to complete missing criteria; do NOT submit until valid

steps.question: Is the adverse reaction serious (death / life-threatening / hospitalisation / disability / congenital anomaly / medically important)?
Is this a clinical trial SUSAR (interventional study)?
steps.if_yes: Submit to EVCTM: 7 days (fatal/life-threatening) or 15 days (other serious). Report to Ethics Committee and CA.
steps.if_no: Submit to EudraVigilance EVPM within 15 calendar days from Day Zero

steps.question: Is the non-serious ICSR from within the EEA?
steps.if_yes: Submit to EudraVigilance EVPM within 90 calendar days from Day Zero
steps.if_no: Store in pharmacovigilance system; NCA submission requirements may vary

follow_up_submission_decision.description: Decision tree for follow-up ICSR submissions

steps.question: Has new information been received about a previously reported ICSR?
Does the new information change seriousness, outcome, causal product, or other key parameter?
steps.if_yes: SIGNIFICANT follow-up: restart clock; submit as follow-up within 15 days (serious) or 90 days (non-serious) from receipt
steps.if_no: NON-SIGNIFICANT follow-up: include in next scheduled update; no clock restart

steps.question: Is the new information purely an administrative correction (data entry error, coding change, no new clinical content)?
steps.if_yes: Submit as AMENDMENT (no clock reset, no new timeline)
steps.if_no: Submit as FOLLOW-UP with new version number and applicable timeline

biological_product_identification.description: Decision tree for biological medicinal product ICSR traceability

steps.question: Is the suspect medicinal product a biological medicinal product?
Are all three identifiers present: brand name + active substance + batch/lot number?
steps.if_no: Attempt follow-up with reporter to obtain missing identifiers; document attempt; include available information

steps.question: Is there more than one batch number involved?
steps.if_yes: Put batch coinciding with adverse reaction in Drug section; list other batch numbers in case narrative
steps.if_no: Include single batch number in Drug section (ICH-E2B(R2) B.4.k.3 or ICH-E2B(R3) G.k.4.r.7)

literature_icsr_day_zero.description: Decision tree for determining Day Zero for literature-sourced ICSRs

steps.question: Does the abstract of the article contain all 4 minimum ICSR validity criteria?
steps.if_yes: Day Zero = date the literature search was run

steps.question: Does obtaining and reading the full article reveal all 4 minimum ICSR validity criteria?
steps.if_yes: Day Zero = date the full article was obtained and reviewed
steps.if_no: Not a valid ICSR; document but do not submit

steps.question: Was a third party contractor aware of the article before the MAH?
steps.if_yes: Day Zero = date third party became aware (earliest awareness triggers clock)
steps.if_no: Day Zero = date MAH became aware
